scope: Pass B full-surface expansion: every endpoint in Algolia's published Search API OpenAPI spec (algolia/api-clients-automation, specs/search/spec.yml) is enumerated below. 7 read streams (indices, api_keys, index_settings, vault_sources, dictionary_settings, dictionary_languages, logs) and 2 writes (update_index_settings, create_api_key) are now covered. Every remaining endpoint is excluded with a specific reason: record/object CRUD and search-query endpoints are a data-plane (not config-plane) surface out of scope for this metadata connector; synonyms/rules/dictionary-entry 'search'-style list endpoints require a POST body the engine's declarative stream dialect cannot send (streams.json's StreamSpec.Body field is accepted by the loader's schema but never read by the read-path engine, read.go's declarative loop always sends a nil body regardless — a real ENGINE_GAP, documented per endpoint below rather than silently worked around); multi-cluster endpoints are deprecated by Algolia's own OpenAPI spec; key/source/dictionary mutation and index copy/move/task-status endpoints are destructive or credential-management admin actions kept out of the reverse-ETL write surface.

excluded.reason: generic custom-request passthrough helper (customRequest.yml), not a resource endpoint of its own
full-text search query execution is a data-plane read (arbitrary query params, not a stable list/detail resource); this connector reads index/key/settings metadata, not search results
multi-index federated search query execution; same data-plane scope narrowing as searchSingleIndex
facet-value search query execution; data-plane, not a metadata resource

bulk record browse/export of index contents; this connector's records are index/key/settings CONFIGURATION, not the underlying search records themselves (a genuinely different, much larger data surface deferred to a future dedicated records connector)
adds/replaces a single search record (addObject); record-level write on the data plane, not a config-plane mutation this connector's writes.json covers
deletes an entire index and all its records/settings
single search-record retrieval; data-plane, not a metadata resource this connector streams
creates/replaces a single search record by objectID; record-level data-plane write
deletes a single search record
bulk-deletes every record matching a query filter; irreversible mass deletion
deletes every record in the index while keeping settings; irreversible
partial record update; record-level data-plane write
batched record add/update/delete operations; record-level data-plane write, and a batch body shape the dialect's single-record write action model doesn't fit
multi-index batched record operations; same data-plane/batch-shape scope narrowing as the single-index batch endpoint
bulk multi-object retrieval by objectID; data-plane read, not a metadata list/detail resource
creates/replaces a single synonym rule; a narrow per-record config-plane write not yet worth a dedicated action alongside searchSynonyms' read gap below
single-synonym detail lookup by objectID, not a list resource; listing synonyms requires searchSynonyms (see below)
deletes a single synonym rule, changing live search relevance for the index
batched synonym save; batch-shape scope narrowing, same as object batch
deletes every synonym rule for the index

ENGINE_GAP: the only way to list ALL synonyms is this POST-with-body 'search' endpoint (empty query returns everything, page/hitsPerPage paginated); the engine's declarative stream dialect has no wired mechanism to send a stream-level request body (streams.json's StreamSpec.Body field is accepted by the schema but never read by the read-path engine -- read.go's declarative loop always sends a nil body) -- implementing this as a real list stream would require either an engine change (wiring stream.Body through) or a Tier-2 StreamHook, both out of scope for this Pass B pass; documented here rather than silently worked around
single-rule detail lookup by objectID, not a list resource; same searchRules POST-body gap as synonyms below
creates/replaces a single query rule; per-record config-plane write
deletes a single query rule, changing live search behavior for the index
batched rule save; batch-shape scope narrowing, same as object batch
deletes every query rule for the index
ENGINE_GAP: identical POST-with-body 'list all rules via empty query' gap as searchSynonyms above -- stream.Body is unwired in the declarative read path
restores a previously-deleted API key; admin-ACL credential-lifecycle action, not a routine reverse-ETL write
single-key detail lookup by key value; api_keys already streams every key's full object from the list endpoint, so this adds no new data, only a redundant per-key path
rotates/reconfigures an existing live API key's ACL and scope; a credential-management action riskier than creating a brand-new key
permanently revokes a live API key; any integration still using it starts failing immediately
batched custom-dictionary-entry save/delete; record-level data-plane write with a batch body shape, not a single-resource mutation this connector's writes.json models
ENGINE_GAP: same POST-with-body 'list custom dictionary entries via empty query' gap as searchSynonyms/searchRules above
toggles standard stop-word dictionary entries on/off per language; a narrow settings-flag write not yet paired with a dedicated action in this pass
Algolia's OpenAPI spec marks every multi-cluster (userID mapping) endpoint deprecated: true
deprecated multi-cluster listUserIds endpoint
deprecated multi-cluster batchAssignUserIds endpoint
deprecated multi-cluster getTopUserIds endpoint
deprecated multi-cluster getUserId endpoint
deprecated multi-cluster removeUserId endpoint
deprecated multi-cluster listClusters endpoint
deprecated multi-cluster searchUserIds endpoint
deprecated multi-cluster hasPendingMappings endpoint
appends a new allowed source IP range to the application's security allowlist; admin-ACL network-security config change
removes an allowed source IP range; can immediately block legitimate traffic from that range if misapplied
polls the status of a single async application-level task by taskID; a taskID is only known transiently right after issuing a mutation, so there is no stable id list to stream against
polls the status of a single async per-index indexing task by taskID; same no-stable-id-list scope narrowing as getAppTask
copies or moves (renames) an entire index, overwriting the destination index's existing data/settings
